one.en: PARENTING – Ex Tempore Reasons – Interim parenting orders – Where the mother seeks to suspend the father’s time and relocate the children’s primary re…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Ex Tempore Reasons – Interim parenting orders – Where the mother seeks to suspend the father’s time and relocate the children’s primary residence from Adelaide to Town B – Where the mother alleges the children are at risk of both physical abuse together with psychological and/or emotional abuse – Where the children have significant and high needs – Where ICL supports the order sought by the mother – Where the Court cannot exclude the possibility that the children have suffered harm – Where the Court is satisfied that it is presently in the children’s best interests to live with the mother and relocate on an interim basis – Orders made for the children to spend supervised time with the father each alternate weekend – Supervision considered suitably protective against the risk of harm the father poses

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J:

INTRODUCTION

These are parenting proceedings in relation to the parties' two children, X, born in 2014, who is 11 years of age, and Y, born in 2017, who is eight years of age.
The proceedings come before the Court as a consequence of an Application in a Proceeding filed by the mother on 23 March 2026 (sealed 24 March 2026). The application has arisen in the context of broader parenting proceedings in which the mother ultimately seeks orders that she be permitted to relocate the children's principal place of residence from Adelaide to Town B.
It appears to be an agreed fact that there was an incident that took place on 17 March 2026 involving the father and the child, X. As a consequence of that incident, the mother now promotes alternate parenting arrangements. The parenting arrangements that have been in place for a number of years in relation to these children, firstly, as a consequence of an agreement reached between the parties following separation and prior to the commencement of these proceedings, and secondly, as a consequence of orders made in these proceedings, is that the children have lived in a week about parenting arrangement between both parties’ households.
The orders that were made in these proceedings in that regard were made on 2 and 3 September 2024 following the first contested interlocutory hearing in these proceedings, those orders being made by a senior judicial registrar.
The orders that the Court is now being invited to make on behalf of the mother provide for the father's time spending with the children to be suspended, that she be permitted to relocate the children to Town B to live with her and that the children communicate on one occasion each week with the father.
During the course of the hearing today and with the question having been asked, the mother now promotes that if she is permitted to relocate the children from Adelaide to Town B, that the children spend time with the father in Adelaide each alternate weekend on either Saturday only or Saturday and Sunday, with that time to be supervised by the maternal aunt, Ms C.
The father's position today is that the mother's Application in a Proceeding be dismissed.
Again, having pressed the father's legal representative during the course of the hearing today, as to the father's position if the Court was to accede to the mother's application, the father has promoted two alternatives: firstly, that there is no need for the children's time with him to be supervised, but secondly, if it was to be supervised, then the father does not oppose the maternal aunt being the supervisor. As an alternative, the father also proposes supervision be effected by the paternal grandmother. The identity of the paternal grandmother as the supervisor, however, is not the subject of agreement.
So far as supervised time spending is concerned from the father's perspective, he equally promotes an alternate weekend regime with that time to occur in Adelaide across both a Saturday and a Sunday. More extensive if the time was unsupervised, less extensive if the time was to be supervised.
It is an agreed position between the parties that one of the factors that the Court would have to bring to account if the children were to relocate to Town B and spend time with the father in Adelaide, in particular, or indeed, if the father was to travel to Town B for time spending, is that it is a six hour drive each way and that necessarily will impact upon the time available on each weekend for the children to spend time with the father.
The Independent Children's Lawyer (“ICL”) promotes orders being made that provide for the children to live with the mother and spend supervised time with the father.
None of the parties today are inviting the Court to make any orders in relation to parental responsibility for these children.

DOCUMENTS BEFORE THE COURT

The documents the Court has before it today are the Application in a Proceeding filed by the mother on 24 March 2026, together with the mother’s affidavit in support of the same, and her Outline of Case filed on 7 April 2026 in preparation for today's hearing.
The father relies on his Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 2 April 2026, together with the affidavit filed in support of the same, and his Outline of Case filed on 7 April 2026 in readiness for today's hearing.
The Independent Children's Lawyer has filed an Outline of Case for today's hearing and that was also filed on 7 April 2026.
In addition to those documents, the Court has generally had regard to the history of the litigation and the documents filed since the inception of the proceedings in May 2024. In addition, the Court has the benefit of a SAPOL report prepared in mid-February 2026, together with, significantly for present purposes, two additional documents. The first is the Family Report prepared by Ms D dated 31 January 2025. The second is an affidavit filed in these proceedings by the mother on 2 April 2025, which has annexed to it a number of reports in relation to the children from their various treating health practitioners.
What should be said in that regard is that there is no dispute between the parties that these children have significant and high needs.
In relation to the child, X, he has been diagnosed with ADHD and learning difficulties, and whilst he has been assessed for Autism Spectrum Disorder, he did not meet the diagnostic criteria for the same. However, he is being monitored and is receiving support from a psychologist and speech pathologist.
In relation to the child, Y, he has a number of diagnoses. More recently, he has been diagnosed with a genetic condition. He has also had a psychoeducational assessment and, as a result of the same, he was diagnosed with an intellectual disability. He has also had speech and language assessments, and he engages with a speech pathologist. All of his needs result in him being eligible for NDIS funding.
All of those reports attached to the affidavit of the mother filed in these proceedings are documents that the Court has had regard to.
It ought to be said from the outset that, in light of the interlocutory nature of these proceedings, the Court is doing the best that it can with the evidence that it presently has before it.
These reasons are being given on an ex tempore basis immediately following argument heard this day. As the Court is permitted, given these are interim parenting proceedings, the Court intends to give short form reasons, although I suspect these reasons will likely be a little more detailed than that.

BACKGROUND

By way of background, the parties began their relationship when they were teenagers in approximately 2010. They separated for a period of time before reconciling and commencing cohabitation in 2013. The child, X, was born, in 2014, and Y was born in 2017.
While there is a dispute between the parties as to the date of separation (on the father's case in 2019 and on the mother's case in April 2020), nothing turns on the date of separation because it appears that the parties remained living together for a period of time following separation, with the mother moving out temporarily in April 2020 before returning to the family home in August 2020 and the mother ultimately moving out on a permanent basis in December 2020 on her case.
The parties participated in dispute resolution following their separation and they agreed to an equal shared parenting regime. Those arrangements prevailed following the parties' separation until events that took place and precipitated the father commencing these proceedings. The events that took place were that in March 2024, the mother withheld the children and, indeed, the father did not spend time with the children until his time was reinstated in the course of these proceedings.
It is an agreed fact that following separation, and in approximately May 2022, the mother began working at E Limited in Town B. It is also an agreed fact that the mother relocated with the children to Town B and it was this fact, coupled with the mother's suspension of the father's time spending, that led to the father commencing these proceedings when he filed his Initiating Application on 22 May 2024.
At the time that the father filed his Initiating Application, X was 9 years of age and Y was six years of age.
At the first contested hearing in these proceedings, before a senior judicial registrar, orders were made requiring the mother to return the children such that they lived in Adelaide on a week about shared care arrangement. In addition, the children were to return to their former school, F School, and orders were made for communication with each parent each Tuesday, Thursday and Saturday, or as requested by the children.
Amongst a number of additional orders that were made, significantly, orders were also made on 2 September 2024 for the preparation of a Family Assessment Report. As earlier indicated, that report was ultimately prepared and that report is before the Court and is dated 31 January 2025.
The orders made in September 2024 have prevailed since that time. That is, until recent events which took place on 17 March 2026, which have again resulted in the mother withholding the children from the father and effectively suspending the children's time spending with him. It is the events of 17 March 2026 which have led to the current Application in the Proceeding filed by the mother. I will return to those events shortly.
So far as the parties' competing historic allegations in these proceedings, the Court is acutely aware that the parties have a range of competing allegations, some relevant to the parenting proceedings and the issues in dispute between them, and others which whilst likely highly relevant to the parties, may well have little impact on the ultimate parenting arrangements for these children. An example of the latter are the allegations made by the father in relation to the mother having extra relationships outside of the parties' relationship.
Of the relevant allegations made by each of the parties, the father, amongst other things, has alleged that the mother has a history of self-harm including on one occasion, as a result of an argument that took place between the parties, the mother attempting to slit her wrists. There are allegations of mutual arguments and the father, at least when the proceedings first commenced, suspected that the mother was suffering from depression and that the mother had been physical with and that she had at least on one occasion hit the children.
From the mother's perspective, she details allegations in relation to the father which are best described as family violence particularly in the form of coercive and controlling behaviour. Picking up on my earlier observation as to matters which may not ultimately be of relevance in relation to the parenting proceedings, however from the mother's perspective, quite possibly relevant, is that the mother, amongst the allegations she makes in relation to the coercive and controlling behaviour by the father, includes the father controlling or attempting to control her friendships and repeatedly making allegations that she was having extra relationships outside of the parties' relationships.
Nothing turns on those matters, for present purposes, other than to acknowledge that they may well be issues to be traversed at any final hearing in these proceedings.
Significantly from the mother's perspective, the events that ultimately led her to withhold the children from spending time with the father, which led to the commencement of these proceedings, are the mother's significant child safety and neglect concerns. The mother has alleged that the father was not attentive to attending to the children's health and their needs. She raises concerns about the children's presentation at school, appropriate food, clothing, failure to administer supplements and medication and matters of that nature.
Indeed, it is the mother's position that, in the lead up to her withholding the children's time spending, the child, X, was diagnosed with pneumonia and an upper respiratory infection, which she considers had worsened as a consequence of the father's inattentiveness to his condition and seeking medical care.
Again, those matters are not matters which the Court can determine at this interlocutory stage, and they may well be matters to be traversed at any final hearing.
Significantly, however, recent events which took place on 17 March 2026 are ones that require more careful consideration by the Court. It is the mother's position that, immediately following the children's return to her care on 17 March 2026, the child, X, made disclosures to her of physical and verbal abuse by the father.
The mother alleges and sets out in some detail in her affidavit the allegations and the disclosures made by X. They include an altercation of some sort between X and the father in which X alleges that the father called him a “thief” and told him that he should not “steal” things from his home (paragraph 5.2). Those allegations included the father removing items from X's bag, X sitting on a chair, the father approaching him, yelling and spitting in X's face, telling X, "I will knock you out, you cunt", said with such force and venom that the father's spit landed on X and he had to wipe the same away (paragraph 5.6).
The incident continued, as alleged by X, with the father grabbing X by his collar, pulling him up from the chair, pushing X to his room, such that X sustained a small graze to his right knee. X disclosed to the mother that he was crying and that the father told him to stop being dramatic and that he was being a “pussy” (paragraph 5.11). The incident continued with, amongst other things, the father motioning with his fist to punch X and X flinching and the father laughing.
In relation to those allegations, the father is on oath in his responding affidavit. The father acknowledges that there was an incident that took place on 17 March 2026. The Court's interpretation as to that incident as deposed by the father is that it was nothing more than appropriate parenting by the father in response to X misbehaving.
The father deposes in his affidavit that, as X was packing his bag to return to the mother and to attend school, the father observed that there was a piece of clothing in the bag that he did not need to take with him. The father admits that he told X he did not need to, "steal from us" (paragraph 10), but he otherwise denies calling X a thief or the mother stupid, as alleged by X.
The father deposes that he was in proximity to both children and that both children began fighting thereafter. He says that he scolded X for fighting with his brother and admits to walking X to his room for a “time out” (paragraph 8). The father additionally admits using his “big voice” in speaking with X on that occasion (paragraph 13).
I will come back to this incident shortly, however, it is this incident which has led to the current circumstances.
The mother has reported the recent disclosures by X to the Department of Child Protection. The father, in his responding affidavit, indicates that he has not had contact from the Department of Child Protection in relation to the incident.
I pause to mention that there is an Independent Children's Lawyer appointed to advance the interests of these children, the ICL having been appointed in these proceedings on 26 March 2025.
In discharge of the ICL's obligations pursuant to s 68L(5A) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), the Independent Children's Lawyer has met with the children on two separate occasions, the first being on 17 July 2025 and the second being very recently on 2 April 2026.
In relation to the more recent meeting, it is observed that that meeting took place after the episode on 17 March 2026. The Independent Children's Lawyer has indicated to the Court today that both children gave a similar account to her, of that deposed by the mother in relation to the incident that took place on 17 March 2026. She additionally indicates that the child, X, told her that his knee was bleeding, that the father was yelling and spitting in his face, and that both children disclosed to her that the father makes derogatory comments to them about themselves, that he gets angry and uses the “C-word” and the “F-word”, and that the father's poor behaviour is mainly directed to the child, X.
In the context of those discussions, the children, in their meeting with the ICL on 2 April 2026, much as they did when they met with the ICL on 17 July 2025, have told the Independent Children's Lawyer that they wish to live with their mother in Town B.
In addition, at their more recent appointment, the children have told the Independent Children's Lawyer that they do not wish to be separated from each other so far as their living and time spending arrangements are concerned.
The recent events of 17 March 2026 must also be seen in the context of matters raised and disclosed again by the child, X, to the mother, reported to police, but additionally raised by the mother and disclosed by the child X during the appointments for the Family Report process. Those disclosures relate to an incident that took place sometime in either late September or early October 2024. In relation to those disclosures, the allegation as made by the mother, as disclosed by X to her, is that the father punched him and caused a bruise to his leg. Those allegations/disclosures were repeated by X when he met with Ms D, and as a result of Ms D's observations and opinion in relation to X's disclosures, in relation to that incident, a mandatory report was made by Ms D.
With reference to the SAPOL information summary report dated early February 2026, the Court understands that, while there remains conjecture between the parties as to what is alleged to have occurred in September/October 2024, ultimately, SAPOL determined that they would not investigate the allegation and disclosure further in circumstances where they had cited material the father produced to them which satisfied SAPOL that the bruise X sustained was as a result of a school fall and not as a result of a direct injury caused by the father.
In relation to that conclusion by SAPOL, and having had regard to the brief record regarding those matters, it is unclear to the Court what actually occurred, what actual disclosures, if any, X might have made to the police, what documents the father showed SAPOL officers to satisfy them that there was an explanation open and acceptable to SAPOL that the injury had occurred as a result of a school fall. That is because, in relation to the latter, the father has not put that evidence before the Court.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Turning to the relevant legislative pathway, I have had regard to Pt VII of the Act and, in particular, ss 60B, 60CC and 60CG of the Act as set out below:
60B Objects of Part
The objects of this Part are:
(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and

(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989.

...

60CC How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests
Determining child’s best interests

(1) Subject to subsection (4), in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2); and
(b) if the child is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child—also consider the matters set out in subsection (3).
General considerations

(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;
(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

...
60CG Court to consider risk of family violence
(1) In considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order:
(a) is consistent with any family violence order; and
(b) does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court may include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
Indeed, I am acutely conscious that, when making any parenting orders in relation to the children, the Court is to have regard to the best interests of the child as the paramount concern.
The Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) was recently amended. Significant amongst those amendments were those amendments made to s 60CC of the Act, namely, the best interests considerations.
Significantly, the general considerations were amended and significantly reduced. Subsection 60CC(2)(a) of the Act is of significance because it mandates that the Court must have regard to the arrangements that would promote the safety, including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm of the child and each person who has the care of the child.
As a result of a combination of the redrafted ss 60CC, 60CG and 60B of the Act, there is no doubt from the Court's perspective that assessment of risk and protecting children from harm and the concomitant safety considerations are at the fore of the Court's consideration in parenting proceedings when determining those arrangements that are in a child’s best interests. Should there be any doubt in that regard, in the recent Full Court decision of Fiedler & Vitale [2025] FedCFamC1A 7 at [54], the Court made the following observation:
...whilst we agree with the submission that no one consideration prescribed by s 60CC(2) is determinative of a child’s best interests, it is difficult to fathom a circumstance in which s 60CC(2)(a) would not be given prominence in the line of reasoning, if the Court was of the view that a child was not safe and/or where the child was at risk of some form of harm...
I am equally conscious that these are interlocutory parenting proceedings. It is difficult for the Court to make findings at an interim stage as to the various allegations made by each of the parties. Indeed, the assessment of risk is one of the more difficult decisions placed on family law decision makers, and often invariably, the assessment of risk cannot wait until the final piece of evidence has been uncovered.
I am conscious of the nature of the assessment of risk as set out by the Full Court in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 in so far as the same involving an assessment of events which are said to have occurred together with a prospective exercise.

DISCUSSION

In the context of these proceedings, and orders made in these proceedings, following a transfer of the proceedings from Division 2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia to this Court, is that at a case management hearing on 28 January 2026, the parties agreed, and indeed an order was made, for the preparation of an updated Family Report by the report writer Ms D with the same to be released no earlier than June 2026.
I mention that because, whilst I will come back to Ms D's first report, it is not lost on the Court that the parties are soon to embark on a further report process, including but not limited to the parents and the children meeting with Ms D, Ms D considering all of the material that has been filed and produced in these proceedings and ultimately forming an opinion as to the parenting arrangements which Ms D, in all her experience, considers to be in the children's best interests.
Whilst a family report is a significant piece of evidence in any parenting proceedings, it is equally understood that the Court is not bound by the recommendations of the report writer. Indeed, the whole purpose of a final hearing is to test all of the evidence, be it the evidence of the parties, their witnesses, but additionally any experts such as Ms D.
So far as Ms D's first report in these proceedings is concerned, Ms D ultimately made a recommendation that the children ought live in the mother's primary care and that they ought spend alternate weekends and school holiday time with the father if the children were to live in Adelaide and if the children were to live in Town B, then time with the father, at most, every three weekends and additionally school holiday time.
It is unclear to the Court as to why a different regime of weekend time spending was proposed if the children were to live in Adelaide as against Town B. That may well be an issue for the updated Family Report the parties are about to embark upon and/or any final hearing.
Additionally, Ms D identified that if the allegations disclosed by X to her (in relation to the bruising allegation) were confirmed, then Ms D considered that any time spending between the children and the father be supervised and that it be reduced until such time that the father completed parenting education.
Ms D also made recommendations about the mother, with Ms D considering that orders were necessary to facilitate the mother accessing supports for the children in a timely manner if, indeed, they were to move to Town B.
Significant in the Family Report prepared by Ms D are her observations and the views she formed that the parents do not have an effective co-parenting relationship and her opinion that the same was having an impact upon the parenting for these children.
At paragraph 132 of her report, Ms D sets out her opinion in that regard as follows:
The parties' account of their co-parenting relationship raises concern, given the

context of the past and current equal time arrangement. They do not demonstrate

collaborate or cooperative co-parenting responses. They are unable to problem solve,

and the mother's unilateral relocation in response to concerns was noted. Equal time

parenting that supports children's relationships and development occurs when

ongoing antagonism does not exist between parents and when they are able to

approach issues and decision making relating to parenting with a civil, constructive

and committed approach. The absence of a shared approach to parenting has likely

resulted in [X] and [Y] moving between two entirely separate households where

they exert considerable emotional energy in managing the significant psychological shift required by such an arrangement, notwithstanding the significant travel that is required to/from school in their mother's care.
Significantly, in relation to the disclosures made to her by X, at paragraph 128 of the Family Report, Ms D discussed the same emphasising the following:
The Court may consider [X's] interview with the Consultant, in which he spontaneously provided contextually detailed information about an alleged instance of physical abuse resulting in injury, with details of this externally consistent with information provided by the mother; that is, [X] seemed a reliable reporter.
Ms D observed that the “only inconsistency lay in [X's] report of timing” but she opined that “it may be that his temporal development is delayed, in line with other aspects of his development” (paragraph 128).
It is the mother's position, as supported by the ICL, that as a result of the disclosures made by X, both that in relation to the incident in late 2024 and the more recent incident in March 2026, that the children are at risk of both physical abuse together with psychological and/or emotional abuse. It is the mother's position, as presumably supported by the ICL in light of the orders the ICL promotes, that the children are at risk of harm such that the harm needs to be protected against. It is the mother's position that the children's safety ought take priority in considering any parenting orders for these children.
That position is supported by the Independent Children's Lawyer. Indeed, the Independent Children's Lawyer has very significant concerns that the children have been subjected to abuse, that they are at risk of emotional and psychological abuse and that they have been subjected to family violence.
In relation to all of those matters, as I earlier indicated, it is difficult for the Court to make any findings. At this juncture, however, it is impossible for the Court to exclude the possibility that these children, and in particular X, have suffered some harm, be it physical and/or emotional and/or psychological abuse, at the hands of their father.
I accept the submissions made on behalf of the mother and the Independent Children's Lawyer that there is a thread of consistency so far as X's disclosures are concerned in relation to the March 2026 incident (as disclosed to the mother by X) and those matters deposed to by the father about that incident.
The thread of consistency includes that, indeed, there was an altercation between X and the father that day. It includes an admission by the father that he told the child he did not need to, "steal from us." It also includes an admission that the father scolded X and that he used his “big voice”.
Whatever the case may be, it does not appear to be in dispute that the incident has had some effect on X.
Amplifying the Court's concerns are those matters disclosed by the children to the Independent Children's Lawyer. The Court, at this stage, accepts the Independent Children's Lawyer's assessment that the disclosures made by the children to her are consistent with those matters set out by the mother in her affidavit.
What these matters and disclosures highlight to the Court is the difficulty with the recent legislative amendments that require the Independent Children's Lawyer to meet with the children to give them the opportunity to express their views.
The events and matters that have fallen from the Independent Children's Lawyer during the course of today's hearing make it clear to the Court that there is a very real potential for the Independent Children's Lawyer to become a witness in these proceedings, given the disclosures the children have made to her. I do not propose to take that matter further other than to raise it as a concern at this stage.
It may be that the parties and the ICL consider it appropriate to reconsider the involvement of this particular Independent Children's Lawyer in all of the circumstances. I express no firm or clear view in that regard, but it may well be something that the parties and the Independent Children's Lawyer need to turn their mind to.
At this juncture, the Court is acutely aware of a number of matters. The effect of the application by the mother is for the Court to give the imprimatur to the mother relocating the children at an interlocutory stage in the proceedings, namely, a stage in which the Court does not have the opportunity to have all of the evidence fully tested.
The Court is acutely aware of what has been said in decisions like Morgan & Miles [2007] FamCA 1230; (2007) FLC 93-343, that identify that decisions about relocation are best left to a final hearing when all the evidence can be tested and findings made, if necessary, and the Court can properly weigh and balance all of the relevant factors in determining an outcome the Court considers to be in the children's best interests.
In considering matters relating to any interim relocation of the children, the Court is acutely aware that any interim relocation would result in a very, very significant disruption to these children. That disruption includes a disruption to their living arrangements from Adelaide to Town B; Adelaide in very real terms being the only place these children have lived. It includes an impact on their schooling, their friendships and irrevocably will mean a change to the children's relationship with their father, given they are no longer living in the same location, that they would need to travel for any time spending with him and that, as a result, any time spending would necessarily be curtailed and, indeed, significantly curtailed from the current week about time spending arrangement.
In addition, for these children who have different but equally high levels of needs and support, any interim relocation will necessarily impact the support that these children receive and potentially has the impact of altering the treating practitioners who provide that support to the children. It is equally not lost on the Court that, so far as the children's schooling arrangements, the children receive internal school support and, again, that would need to change. All of that is a way of saying that there would be a very significant disruption to these children if they were permitted to relocate to Town B to live primarily with the mother.
In addition to those matters, the Court is acutely aware that their father has re-partnered and that he has two children from his new relationship. Those children are young. G was born on in 2024 and H was born in 2026. Those two children live in the father's home. They are these children's siblings and any relocation of the children to Town B will necessarily impact the relationship that these children have with their siblings who live with the father.
In addition, the Court has had regard to the possibility that some or all of the allegations made by the mother may not be made out at any final hearing or, indeed, that the Court may be unable to make findings about those matters at any final hearing.
Ultimately, however, the focus of the Court at this juncture must be on the assessment of risk and with a focus on protecting the children from harm and their safety as the Court's primary concern.
Additional factors which are weighing on the Court's mind at this juncture, if the Court was to accede to the mother's application, is that the Court cannot, at this juncture, exclude the possibility that the mother, by her own conduct and/or comments to the children, has had some influence and impact on the children and the views that they have expressed to the Independent Children's Lawyer, that they wish to live primarily with the mother in Town B.
Allegations have been made by the father that the mother's position has been motivated by financial issues and, in particular, he has pointed to the number of occasions in which the mother has sought a change to the administrative assessment of child support as a consequence of her withholding the children but, in addition, her applications to have non-agency payments accounted for in the assessment of child support.
I accept, however, at this preliminary stage, that the Court is, firstly, unable to make findings about the mother's motivation in that regard without hearing the evidence of each of the parties but also the submissions made on behalf of the mother that these are not affluent people and that every penny for these people counts, particularly where these children have high needs and the mother is meeting those needs while the children are in her care.
Another criticism made by the father of the mother, which has exercised the concern of the Independent Children's Lawyer, relates to the housing arrangements for the children when they are living with the mother in Adelaide.
The Court understands that the mother presently remains living in Town B with her partner and that when the children are in her care, she travels to Adelaide and lives with her sister, the maternal aunt, Ms C, who is the mother's proposed supervisor, in her home together with her partner and their child, the subject children's cousin.
The Court is told that the mother has her own room in that accommodation and that the children additionally have their own room. The concern from the father and the Independent Children's Lawyer is that that housing arrangement is not stable, the children do not have all of their personal belongings and that it is a temporary arrangement. There is some merit in that criticism made of the mother. However, the degree to which that issue has an impact today is nominal in circumstances where the Court understands that the mother has implemented that housing arrangement to ensure that she remains in her present employment, deriving an income in which she is able to support the children.
So far as the mother's employment is concerned, an issue and a concern has arisen from the father's perspective as to the mother maintaining her employment in Town B as against seeking employment in Adelaide to facilitate the children living in Adelaide regardless of the outcome of today's application, and the mother's position that the children ought primarily live with her.
The reality, however, at this stage, is that the Court does not have evidence beyond the father's speculation and an understanding that the mother previously did live and work in Adelaide, as to her employment options in Adelaide. The reality of the circumstances from the mother's perspective, at least at this stage, is that she wishes to continue to live in Town B where her partner also lives and where they are both working for E Limited.
Concerns have been raised by the father in relation to the children's poor school attendance and lateness while in the mother's care. Other than to note those concerns, no findings, at this stage can be made in that regard. Again, that is an issue that will need to be traversed at any final hearing.
The overarching concern of the Court at this juncture are the safety issues that have been raised and which are exercising the mind of the mother and, significantly, the Independent Children's Lawyer. At this juncture, it is difficult for the Court to exclude the possibility that the children have suffered harm, particularly the child, X, has suffered physical harm, and that both children have suffered psychological and/or emotional harm.
The incident of 17 March 2026 and the disclosures made to the Independent Children's Lawyer about that incident by the children heighten the Court's concerns in that regard. The previous incident that took place in either September or October 2024 adds fuel to those concerns, albeit that there is uncertainty about those events. It is difficult, in all of those circumstances, for the Court to ignore the potential that these children have already suffered harm and that they are at risk of suffering ongoing and/or further harm in the future.
Cases like the present are difficult to determine, whether it be at an interlocutory or a final hearing. The difficulty for the Court at this interlocutory stage is that if the Court did not act protectively of these children in circumstances where concerns exist that the children have suffered harm and may suffer harm in the future in the father's care are such that it is difficult at an interlocutory stage to ignore the allegations that have been made in this matter.
That has a corollary and a consequence because if, ultimately, at any final hearing, the Court is not satisfied that the father presents a risk of harm to these children for whatever reasons the Court considers that to be so, any arrangement which sees the curtailing of the children's relationship with the father now will have an impact on these children and an impact on the ultimate parenting arrangements for these children.
It is not lost on the Court that if, indeed, the Court accedes to the mother's application today, and ultimately, the Court is not satisfied at any final hearing that the father presents a risk of harm to these children, then there is the potential for further disruption to these children if they were ordered to return to live primarily in Adelaide.
All of those matters make decisions such as the one the Court is being asked to make today a very difficult decision. There are no clear answers. On balance, however, the Court is of the view that it is appropriate to take protective measures to ensure these children's safety. Those protective measures to ameliorate the risk of harm that the Court considers the father likely presents are that supervised time spending is the most appropriate arrangement at this juncture because it is suitably protective against the risk of harm the father poses.
That necessarily will mean that the children's time spending arrangements with the father must change.
The Court cannot ignore that the mother lives part-time in Town B and part-time in Adelaide. It is impossible for the mother to live full-time in Adelaide and to maintain her employment. Leaving aside her housing arrangements, and on the assumption that her housing arrangements in Adelaide with her sister would not be an issue, the mother's employment is a significant issue at this juncture.
For all of those reasons, and whilst finely balanced, the Court is of the view that the children should live with the mother and that the same necessarily will result in the children being relocated to live with the mother in Town B.
So far as the appropriate time spending arrangements for the children to maintain their relationship with the father, those time spending arrangements will be supervised as agreed between the parties by Ms C and time will take place each alternate weekend on both Saturday and Sunday for a period of three hours, facilitating the children travelling six hours to Adelaide on a Saturday morning and returning, again, a six-hour drive to Town B on a Sunday afternoon.
In addition to orders that provide for the earlier orders regarding the children's living arrangements to be discharged, orders will be made that provide for the children to live with the mother, that she be permitted to relocate their principal place of residence to Town B and that the children spend time with the father in Adelaide each alternate weekend on both Saturday and Sunday alternating between the maternal aunt's and the father's residence.
In addition to those orders and again by agreement between the parties, orders will be made as promoted by the Independent Children's Lawyer in the draft minute of order attached to their Outline of Case, namely paragraphs 10 to 18 (inclusive).
NOTE:

These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been settled from the transcript. Grammatical errors have been corrected, citations have been added and amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clearer and easier to read.
